text.title: Stakeholder Engagement — Framework and Process
text.summary: Five-step engagement process, Power/Interest grid, quadrant strategies, and the communication matrix pattern.

list.item: Step 1: Identify all stakeholders — sponsor, users, customers, team, regulators, suppliers, influencers. Seed from org chart, charter, RFP, and contract.
Step 2: Analyze Power/Interest — map each stakeholder on the 2x2 grid; apply Mitchell-Agle-Wood (power × legitimacy × urgency) for political risk.
Step 3: Plan engagement strategies — one strategy per quadrant; override only with explicit rationale.
Step 4: Execute communication plan — cadence, channel, owner, content per stakeholder.
Step 5: Monitor and adapt — refresh quarterly minimum; triangulate self-reported attitude with behavioural signals.

rule.statement: Apply the default cadence by quadrant: Manage Closely = weekly 1:1, Keep Satisfied = biweekly summary, Keep Informed = monthly digest, Monitor = release notes only.
rule.rationale: Default cadence is empirically calibrated to stakeholder tolerance; deviating without rationale causes either fatigue (over-engagement) or surprises (under-engagement).

list.item: High Power + High Interest (Manage Closely): weekly meetings, direct updates, real-time dashboard access, early warning on delays.
High Power + Low Interest (Keep Satisfied): monthly briefings, escalation path, fast response to queries, no unnecessary meetings.
Low Power + High Interest (Keep Informed): newsletters, status reports, sprint demos where relevant.
Low Power + Low Interest (Monitor): general announcements, release notes.

rule.statement: Require an evidence field on every attitude assertion — no evidence means attitude = "unknown", not "supportive".
rule.rationale: LLMs over-classify everyone as "supportive" by default (politeness bias). Forcing evidence eliminates false positives that lead to missed resistors.

rule.statement: Triangulate self-reported attitude with at least one behavioural signal: meeting attendance, response time, escalations, or Slack sentiment.
rule.rationale: People say "supportive" in workshops and undermine in private; behavioural signals are less gameable than self-report.

antipattern.description: Treating all Manage Closely stakeholders with the same tone and cadence.
antipattern.reason: A CFO and a regulator demand opposite communication styles even though both fall in the same quadrant — context-specific strategy is required.

rule.statement: Always include a hidden stakeholder pass: legal, infosec, procurement, works council/union, accessibility, downstream API consumers.
rule.rationale: Systematically missed stakeholders are the top source of late-stage blockers in enterprise programs; they never appear in org-chart exports.

text.reference: Stakeholder register schema with all required fields
Script to flag stakeholders past their engagement cadence
